# Foam Paint — Auto-Generated Knowledge Graph for Any Project

## What This Is

A system that automatically tracks how every file in a repo connects to every
other file — and keeps that map current without manual maintenance. Six
components:

1. **`KB_INDEX.md`** — An auto-generated index at repo root. Lists every
   production module and doc, its imports/dependencies, what depends on it, and
   wiki-link cross-references. Agents read this first to understand what exists
   and what touches what. **Never edited by hand.**

2. **`scripts/kb_graph.py`** — A Python CLI (stdlib only) that scans the repo,
   parses `import` statements in code and `[[wiki-links]]` in markdown, builds
   a directed dependency graph, and outputs KB_INDEX.md. Also provides query
   commands for blast-radius analysis.

3. **`graph.html`** — An interactive D3.js visualization of the dependency graph.
   Generated by the `visualize` command. Dark-themed force-directed layout with
   click-to-inspect blast radius. Gitignored (generated artifact). Humans use
   this to browse connections and spot orphans visually.

4. **Foam** — A VS Code extension that visualizes the wiki-link graph
   interactively. The human uses this to browse connections and spot orphans.

5. **`.git/hooks/pre-commit`** — Runs `kb_graph.py rebuild-index` before every
   commit, stages the updated KB_INDEX.md, and includes it in the same commit.
   The index is always in sync — never stale, never one commit behind.

6. **`/check_graph`** — A Claude Code skill that the agent can invoke
   autonomously or the user can trigger explicitly. Spawns an agent to run
   graph queries, read affected files, and return a structured impact report
   without bloating the main conversation context.

## Why

When a project has 10+ docs and growing code, changes cascade. A function rename
touches a design doc, an API spec, two scripts, and a config. Without a graph,
Claude (or the developer) guesses which files are affected and misses
connections.

Manually maintaining an index doesn't work when agents are doing the building —
no one remembers to update it, and it drifts from reality within days.

This system makes the graph a **derived artifact**. The source of truth is:
- `import` / `from...import` statements in code files
- `[[wiki-links]]` in markdown docs and `# Design: [[link]]` comments in code
- File existence in tracked directories

`kb_graph.py` reads these and generates everything. The pre-commit hook keeps it
current. The graph grows organically as the codebase grows — no seeding, no
manual upkeep.

## Prerequisites

- Python 3.10+ (stdlib only — no external deps)
- Git
- Claude Code CLI
- VS Code (for Foam visualization — optional but recommended)

## What Gets Built

```
<your-repo>/
  KB_INDEX.md                          # Auto-generated index (never hand-edit)
  graph.html                           # Interactive D3 visualization (gitignored)
  CLAUDE.md                            # Rules: read index, use wiki-links, query graph
  scripts/kb_graph.py                  # Graph CLI — the engine
  scripts/hooks/pre-commit             # Runs rebuild-index, stages KB_INDEX.md
  .claude/skills/check_graph/SKILL.md  # /check_graph skill (agent-invocable)
  .vscode/settings.json                # Foam config (create or merge into existing)
```

---

## Build Steps

### Step 1: Create `scripts/kb_graph.py`

This is the core engine. One Python file, stdlib only. It must support these
CLI commands:

```bash
# Regenerate KB_INDEX.md from current repo state
python scripts/kb_graph.py rebuild-index

# Show direct neighbors of a node (depth 1)
python scripts/kb_graph.py neighbors <node>

# Traverse from a node to depth N, show full blast radius
python scripts/kb_graph.py traverse <node> --depth 2

# Find shortest path between two nodes
python scripts/kb_graph.py path <from> <to>

# List files with no inbound or outbound edges (orphans)
python scripts/kb_graph.py orphans

# Print graph stats (node count, edge count, most-connected nodes)
python scripts/kb_graph.py analyze

# Generate interactive HTML visualization (graph.html)
python scripts/kb_graph.py visualize
```

#### 1a. Configuration constants

These must be customized per project. Defaults below are for a Python project:

```python
# Directories to skip during scanning
IGNORE_DIRS = {".venv", "node_modules", ".git", "__pycache__", "data"}

# Root directories to scan (customize for your repo layout)
SCAN_ROOTS = ["src", "docs"]

# File extensions the graph should track
MD_EXTS = {".md"}
CODE_EXTS = {".py"}                    # Add ".R", ".sh", ".nf", etc. for your stack
CONFIG_EXTS = {".yaml", ".yml", ".json", ".toml"}
ASSET_EXTS = {".png", ".jpg", ".svg", ".pdf"}

ALL_EXTS = MD_EXTS | CODE_EXTS | CONFIG_EXTS | ASSET_EXTS
```

**Choosing SCAN_ROOTS — what to include vs exclude:**

Include directories that contain **production code and planning docs** — the
files agents need to understand interconnections for before writing code.
Exclude directories that are tooling, test code, generated output, or reference
material:

| Include | Exclude |
|---------|---------|
| `src/` — production code | `scripts/` — repo tooling (kb_graph.py itself) |
| `docs/` — implementation plans | `tests/` — test code (no production deps on it) |
| `lib/` — internal libraries | `docs/reference/` — external reference stubs |
| | `docs/specs/` — foundational specs (static, rarely changed) |
| | `build/`, `dist/` — generated output |

**Rule of thumb:** if an agent editing file A needs to know about file B's
existence and connections, both should be in SCAN_ROOTS. If file B is tooling
that supports the repo but isn't part of the production dependency chain, exclude
it. You can always add directories back later if orphan analysis shows gaps.

Use `IGNORE_DIRS` for subdirectories within SCAN_ROOTS that should be skipped
(e.g., `docs/reference/` when `docs/` is a scan root).

#### 1b. Parsers

The graph has **nodes** (files) and **directed edges** (file A depends on
file B). Three parser types extract edges:

**Python parser** — extract from `.py` files:
- `import mypackage.utils.helpers` → edge to `mypackage/utils/helpers.py`
- `from mypackage.config import get_config` → edge to `mypackage/config.py`
- `from .validator import validate` (relative import) → resolve to sibling file
- `# Design: [[some-doc#section]]` → edge to doc
- Ignore stdlib and third-party imports (anything not resolvable to a local
  file in SCAN_ROOTS)
- Extract the first docstring or `# comment` after the module docstring as the
  file description

**Markdown parser** — extract from `.md` files:
- `[[filename]]` → edge to the matching file (search by basename without .md)
- `[[filename#section-slug]]` → edge to file (store section as edge metadata)
- `[[src/utils.py]]` → edge to code file (extension present = code reference)
- Extract the first `#` heading as the file description

**Config parser** — extract from `.yaml`/`.json`/`.toml` files:
- Scan string values for file paths that match tracked files
- Lightweight — don't over-parse, just catch obvious path references

**Adding other languages**: Write a parser function per language. The parser
receives a file path, returns a list of edges. Examples:
- **R**: Parse `source("path")`, `library()` (local packages only)
- **Shell**: Parse `source`, `. path`, script invocations
- **Snakemake/Nextflow**: Parse `include:` rules, `input:`/`output:` file refs

#### 1c. Graph data structure

```python
# Each node is keyed by its path relative to repo root
# e.g. "src/api/routes.py"
graph = {
    "nodes": {
        "src/api/routes.py": {
            "description": "REST API route definitions",
            "package": "api",             # component group, for KB_INDEX sections and viz colors
            "type": "code",               # code | doc | config | asset
        },
        ...
    },
    "edges": [
        {
            "from": "src/api/routes.py",
            "to": "src/core/database.py",
            "type": "import",             # import | wiki-link | config-ref
        },
        ...
    ]
}
```

**Component-level grouping (`package` field):**

The `package` field controls how nodes are grouped in KB_INDEX.md section
headers and how they are colored in the visualization. Group by **logical
component**, not by immediate parent directory. Too-granular grouping (one
color per subdirectory) creates visual noise; too-broad grouping (everything
is one color) loses information.

Target ~8-10 groups. For a typical Python project:

```python
def get_package(filepath):
    parts = filepath.split("/")
    if parts[0] == "docs":
        return "docs"
    if parts[0] == "src":
        if len(parts) == 2:
            return "src-root"          # top-level files like main.py
        return parts[1]                # api, core, workers, etc.
    return parts[0]                    # fallback
```

For projects with deeper nesting (e.g., `src/pipelines/ingest/tools/`), map
sub-packages to their parent component:

```python
if parts[1] == "pipelines":
    if len(parts) == 3:
        return "pipelines-root"        # base.py, __init__.py
    return parts[2]                    # ingest, transform, export
```

The visualization palette should have one distinct color per group. With 10
groups, pick colors that are visually distinguishable at a glance on a dark
background:

```python
palette = ["#58a6ff", "#3fb950", "#d29922", "#f85149", "#bc8cff",
           "#f778ba", "#39d2c0", "#ff9a3c", "#56d364", "#e06c9f"]
```

#### 1d. `rebuild-index` output format

The `rebuild-index` command writes `KB_INDEX.md` at repo root. Format:

```markdown
# KB_INDEX.md
> Auto-generated by `scripts/kb_graph.py rebuild-index` — do not edit manually.
> Last rebuilt: 2026-04-01 14:32:07 UTC | Nodes: 24 | Edges: 41 | Orphans: 0

---

## src/core

- **database.py** — connection pool and query helpers
  - imports: `config.settings`
  - used by: `api.routes`, `api.auth`, `workers.ingest`

- **config.py** — used by 9 modules

## src/api

- **routes.py** — REST API route definitions
  - imports: `core.database`, `core.config`, `.auth`, `.serializers`
  - used by: `main`
  - docs: [[api-design#endpoints]]

## docs

- **api-design.md** — API design spec
  - links to: [[routes.py]], [[auth.py]], [[database.py]]
```

Key rules for the output:
- Group by component (matching the `package` field), not alphabetically by full
  path. Section headers should be human-readable directory-style paths
  (e.g., `src/api` not `api`).
- Heavily-imported modules (used by 5+) show dependent count, not full list
- Code files show: description, imports (local only), used-by, docs (wiki-links)
- Doc files show: description, links-to (wiki-links to code and other docs)
- Header includes stats: node count, edge count, orphan count, timestamp
- When a filename appears more than once in a group (e.g., multiple
  `__init__.py` files), disambiguate with the parent directory prefix
  (e.g., `tools/__init__.py` vs `api/__init__.py`)

#### 1e. `traverse` output format (blast radius)

The traverse command shows the dependency tree **and** greps each file in the
radius for lines that actually reference the target node. This gives the agent
exact line numbers, not just file names.

```
BLAST RADIUS: core/database.py

Depth 0 (the change)
  └── core/database.py

Depth 1 (direct dependents — MUST review)
  ├── api/routes.py ────── imports database
  │     L12: from core.database import get_connection, execute_query
  │     L47: conn = get_connection()
  │     L63: rows = execute_query(conn, sql)
  ├── api/auth.py ─────── imports database
  │     L8:  from core.database import get_connection
  │     L31: conn = get_connection()
  ├── workers/ingest.py ── imports database
  │     L5:  from core import database
  │     L22: database.execute_query(conn, insert_sql)
  └── config/settings.py ─ imported BY database

Depth 2 (transitive — CHECK for breakage)
  ├── main.py ───────────── imports api.routes
  ├── api/serializers.py ── imported by api.routes
  └── docs/api-design.md ── wiki-links to [[database.py]]

Summary: 1 changed → 4 direct → 3 transitive | Risk: HIGH (3 packages in radius)
```

Implementation: for each file in the blast radius, run the equivalent of
`grep -n "<node_basename>" <file>` and include matching lines (capped at 5
per file to keep output readable). This is stdlib — just `open()` + string
matching, no external deps.

Risk is computed by: how many distinct top-level packages/directories appear in
the blast radius. 1 = LOW, 2 = MEDIUM, 3+ = HIGH.

#### 1f. `visualize` command (interactive HTML graph)

The `visualize` command generates a self-contained `graph.html` at repo root.
This file should be gitignored (generated artifact, can be large).

**Requirements:**
- Load D3.js from CDN (`https://d3js.org/d3.v7.min.js`)
- Dark theme background (`#0d1117`)
- Force-directed layout with drag, zoom, and collision detection
- Nodes colored by component group (same `package` field used in KB_INDEX)
- Node radius scaled by degree (more connections = larger)
- Three edge styles: solid for imports, dashed for wiki-links, dotted for
  config-refs
- Click a node to: highlight its blast radius (depth 2), dim unrelated nodes,
  show an info panel with imports, used-by, and risk assessment
- Click background to reset
- Legend showing: component colors (swatches) and edge type styles
- Stats bar: node count, edge count, orphan count

**Implementation:** Embed the graph data as a JSON literal in the HTML. The
`cmd_visualize` function builds the graph, serializes it to JSON, and string-
replaces placeholders in an HTML template. The template contains all CSS and
JavaScript inline.

The legend is generated server-side from the component groups. The palette in
the template JavaScript must match the palette used for legend generation.

---

### Step 2: Create pre-commit hook

Create `scripts/hooks/pre-commit`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# pre-commit — regenerate KB_INDEX.md before every commit
#
# Runs kb_graph.py rebuild-index and stages the result so KB_INDEX.md
# is always in sync with the committed code.

# Only run if code files or markdown files are being committed
STAGED=$(git diff --cached --name-only --diff-filter=ACM)
HAS_CODE=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -cE '\.(py|R|sh|nf)$' || true)
HAS_MD=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -c '\.md$' || true)

if [ "$HAS_CODE" -gt 0 ] || [ "$HAS_MD" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "[pre-commit] Rebuilding KB_INDEX.md..."
    python scripts/kb_graph.py rebuild-index
    git add KB_INDEX.md
    echo "[pre-commit] KB_INDEX.md updated and staged."
fi
```

Then configure git to use this hooks directory:

```bash
chmod +x scripts/hooks/pre-commit
git config core.hooksPath scripts/hooks
```

Storing hooks in `scripts/hooks/` (not `.git/hooks/`) means they are tracked by
git and shared with collaborators automatically. The `core.hooksPath` config is
per-clone — add it to your repo's setup instructions.

---

### Step 3: Create `/check_graph` skill

Create `.claude/skills/check_graph/SKILL.md`:

```markdown
Analyze the impact of a proposed change by querying the project knowledge graph.

Change description: $ARGUMENTS

## Instructions

Spawn an Explore agent to do the heavy lifting. This keeps the main context clean.

Use the Agent tool with subagent_type "Explore" and the following prompt:

---

You are analyzing the impact of a proposed change in this project.

**Change description**: $ARGUMENTS

**Your task**:

1. Run `python scripts/kb_graph.py rebuild-index` to ensure KB_INDEX.md is
   current.

2. Read KB_INDEX.md to understand the project structure.

3. Identify which modules/systems the change involves. Map them to entry-point
   files in the index.

4. For each entry point, run blast-radius queries:
   ```
   python scripts/kb_graph.py traverse <entry-point> --depth 2
   python scripts/kb_graph.py neighbors <entry-point>
   ```

5. Read every affected file the graph reveals. Do not skip any.

6. If the change involves creating a new module that parallels an existing one
   in the repo (e.g., a new `qc.py` in a sibling directory where one already
   exists), identify the existing analog as a **Template** in your report. The
   implementing agent should read the template before writing the new module.

7. Return a structured impact report:

### Change Summary
One sentence.

### Entry Points
Primary files that need edits. List each with its graph key.

### Blast Radius (via graph)
Files surfaced by traversal, grouped by depth, with WHY each is relevant.

### Template (if applicable)
Existing in-repo analog that the new module should follow. Include file path and
the key patterns to replicate (function signatures, return types, how it's wired
into its parent orchestrator/caller).

### Blockers & Decisions
- OPEN: questions that must be resolved before implementation
- DECIDED: existing decisions that constrain this change (quote the source)

### Impact Assessment
- Scope: Isolated (1-2 files) | Moderate (3-5) | Wide (6+)
- Breaking: Yes/No
- Risk: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH (based on how many packages are in blast radius)
- Complexity: Simple | Moderate | Complex

### Recommended Approach
3-5 bullet plan.

### New Wiki-Links Needed
Specific [[wiki-links]] to add to connect this change into the graph.

---

Once the agent returns, display its report directly to the user.
```

---

### Step 4: Add CLAUDE.md rules

Add these rules to the project `CLAUDE.md` (create if it doesn't exist):

```markdown
## Knowledge Graph Rules

1. **Read KB_INDEX.md first.** Before adding, modifying, or looking up content,
   read the knowledge-base index to find the right files and understand their
   connections. KB_INDEX.md is auto-generated — never edit it by hand.

2. **Wiki-links in docs.** When editing any `.md` file, add `[[wiki-links]]` to
   cross-reference related docs and code. Use `[[filename]]` or
   `[[filename#section-slug]]` syntax. In Python files, use
   `# Design: [[doc-name#section]]` comments to link back to design docs.

3. **Query the graph before implementing.** Before starting a feature that
   touches multiple systems, run
   `python scripts/kb_graph.py traverse <node> --depth 2` to discover all
   affected files. Read every file that surfaces before writing code.
   Alternatively, use `/check_graph <description>` to get a full impact report.
```

---

### Step 5: Install Foam + `.vscode/settings.json`

```
code --install-extension foam.foam-vscode
```

Create `.vscode/settings.json` (or merge into existing):

```json
{
  "foam.edit.linkReferenceDefinitions": "withoutExtensions",
  "foam.files.ignore": [
    ".venv/**",
    "node_modules/**",
    "data/**",
    "__pycache__/**"
  ]
}
```

Adjust the ignore list for directories in your project that contain large or
generated files (build outputs, data directories, vendored deps, etc.).

---

### Step 6: Seed wiki-links and connect plan docs to code

Go through existing markdown files and add `[[wiki-links]]` where cross-
references exist. Focus on:

- Main design/architecture docs — add links to the code modules they describe
- Implementation plans — add links to the code files and specs they reference
- Any doc that mentions another doc or code file by name

In code files, add `# Design: [[doc-name]]` comments where a module implements
something described in a doc:

```python
# Design: [[api-design#authentication]]
def verify_token(token: str) -> User:
    ...
```

**Bidirectional plan↔code linking:**

Implementation plan docs (e.g., `docs/api/plan.md`) should link to the code
files they describe. Code files should link back via `# Design:` comments.
This creates a bidirectional cluster that lets agents traverse from a plan to
all the code it governs, and from code back to the plan that specifies its
behavior.

Example — a plan doc for an API component:

```markdown
# API — Implementation Plan

**Code modules:** [[src/api/routes.py]] | [[src/api/auth.py]] | [[src/api/serializers.py]]

## Phase 1 — Authentication
Build [[src/api/auth.py]] with JWT verification...
```

And the corresponding code file:

```python
# Design: [[plan]]
"""JWT authentication middleware."""
...
```

When the agent runs `kb_graph.py traverse docs/api/plan.md --depth 1`, it sees
all the code files the plan touches. When it runs
`kb_graph.py traverse src/api/auth.py --depth 1`, it sees the plan doc that
governs that module. Both directions are useful for understanding impact before
making changes.

**Wiki-link resolution for ambiguous names:** When multiple files share the same
basename (e.g., several `orchestrator.py` files in different packages), use
full path-style wiki-links to be unambiguous:
`[[src/api/routes.py]]` instead of `[[routes.py]]`. The resolver treats links
containing both `/` and `.` as direct path references.

This step is manual the first time. After that, the CLAUDE.md rules ensure
agents add wiki-links as they work.

---

### Step 7: Run initial builds

```bash
python scripts/kb_graph.py rebuild-index
python scripts/kb_graph.py analyze
python scripts/kb_graph.py orphans
python scripts/kb_graph.py visualize
```

Verify KB_INDEX.md was created and looks correct. Open graph.html in a browser
to verify the visualization matches the graph data — same nodes, same edges,
same color groups.

Add `graph.html` to `.gitignore` (generated artifact).

Commit KB_INDEX.md. From this point forward, the pre-commit hook keeps it
current automatically.

---

## How It Works Day-to-Day

### Hook workflow

```
 Plan feature          Agent reads           /check_graph            Agent writes
 (phased docs)  ──►  KB_INDEX.md    ──►   blast radius report  ──►  code
                     (CLAUDE.md rule)      (skill)
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                                                                  git commit
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                                                              ┌─── pre-commit ───┐
                                                              │ kb_graph.py      │
                                                              │ rebuild-index    │
                                                              │ git add KB_INDEX │
                                                              └──────────────────┘
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                                                              Commit includes
                                                              updated KB_INDEX.md
```

### Human workflow

- Plan features in `docs/{feature}/` with phased markdown files
- Link plan docs to code modules with `[[wiki-links]]`
- Run `/check_graph <feature description>` to get impact report
- Browse the Foam graph in VS Code or open graph.html in a browser
- Commit normally — KB_INDEX.md updates itself

### Agent workflow

- Reads KB_INDEX.md first (CLAUDE.md rule) to find relevant files
- Runs `kb_graph.py traverse` before multi-file changes
- Adds `[[wiki-links]]` when editing docs (CLAUDE.md rule)
- Adds `# Design: [[doc]]` comments when implementing from a spec
- Commits — pre-commit hook regenerates KB_INDEX.md automatically

### The graph grows organically

Every time a code file adds an import or a doc adds a wiki-link, the graph gets
a new edge. `kb_graph.py orphans` catches files that fell out of the graph. No
pre-population needed — start from the existing codebase and let it grow.

---

## Customization checklist

When setting up for a new project:

- [ ] Adjust `IGNORE_DIRS` and `SCAN_ROOTS` for your repo layout (production
      code + plan docs in, tooling + tests + reference material out)
- [ ] Adjust `CODE_EXTS` for your languages (`.py`, `.R`, `.sh`, `.nf`, etc.)
- [ ] Write code parsers for your stack (Python imports, R `source()`, etc.)
- [ ] Implement `get_package()` to group files by logical component (~8-10
      groups), not by immediate parent directory
- [ ] Pick a visualization palette with one distinct color per component group
- [ ] Adjust `.vscode/settings.json` Foam ignore patterns
- [ ] Adjust the `/check_graph` agent prompt for your domain
- [ ] Add the 3 CLAUDE.md rules
- [ ] Set `git config core.hooksPath scripts/hooks`
- [ ] Add `graph.html` to `.gitignore`
- [ ] Seed wiki-links in existing docs, including bidirectional plan↔code links
- [ ] Run `rebuild-index` and `visualize`, verify the outputs match
